content.claim: Cross-national quasi-experimental evidence from Hanushek & Woessmann and a 2021 meta-analysis of 75 countries finds that early tracking increases educational inequality with unclear or negative effects on mean performance, and early tracking at ages 10-12 is more strongly linked to inequality than tracking at ages 15-16 applied in most OECD countries.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Finnish system reform lens
limitations: Effect-size estimates and mechanisms remain contested across studies due to different data, domains, and inequality measures
Cross-national studies cannot fully account for institutional context variation between Hungary and comparators
Causal mechanisms linking age-of-tracking to inequality magnitude are not fully resolved in literature
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
